items.q: Why does deforestation increase flood risk?
items.correct: Trees intercept rainfall, their roots increase infiltration and transpiration removes water — removing them increases surface runoff and reduces lag time, peaking river levels faster.
items.misconception: Deforestation causes floods because trees normally hold water in their branches forever.
items.random_wrong: Deforestation has no effect on rivers — only rainfall matters.
items.why: Interception is temporary but it delays and reduces runoff. Without forests, water reaches rivers faster and in greater volume — interacting with antecedent soil moisture to drive flood peaks.

items.q: What is the difference between biotic and abiotic components of an ecosystem?
items.correct: Biotic components are all living organisms (plants, animals, microbes); abiotic components are non-living factors (climate, soil, water, light, rock).
items.misconception: Biotic means animals and abiotic means plants.
items.random_wrong: Biotic and abiotic both refer to types of soil.
items.why: Plants are biotic, not abiotic. Students sort by familiarity rather than living vs non-living. The distinction matters because abiotic factors set the conditions for which biotic communities develop.

items.q: Why is HDI a better measure of development than GNI per capita?
items.correct: HDI combines income, life expectancy and education, capturing quality of life — GNI per capita is just average income and hides inequality and non-economic wellbeing.
items.misconception: Because HDI measures how happy people are.
items.random_wrong: Because HDI is easier to calculate than GNI.
items.why: Students treat HDI as a happiness index. It is broader than GNI but still only captures three measurable dimensions — not subjective wellbeing or environmental quality.

items.q: What is the BIGGEST single cause of deforestation in the Brazilian Amazon?
items.why: It's Cattle ranching. “Mining” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Why is the SOIL in a tropical rainforest typically infertile if the trees are removed?
items.correct: Heavy rain leaches nutrients without trees to cycle them
items.why: Correct answer: Heavy rain leaches nutrients without trees to cycle them. “It is too cold” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Roughly what percentage of WORLD species live in tropical rainforests?
items.why: Exact answer: 50. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
